blocks.text: Happy are those who do not follow the advice of the wicked, or take the path that sinners tread, or sit in the seat of scoffers;

Ps 1: The two ways. A torah or instructional psalm serving as an introduction to the Psalter, along with Ps 2. Both celebrate the saving presence of God—Ps 1 for the individual and Ps 2 for the Davidic king—who offers blessing and protection from the wicked. Unlike the following psalms, these introductory works lack a Davidic superscription, suggesting their secondary, editorial function. Using the metaphor of the two ways, one leading to life and the other to death, Psalm 1 declares that reciting God’s saving torah or teaching (in this case, including the psalms) keeps one on the path of life.
1 : , like and , contemptuously dismiss God’s capacity to rule the world.
but their delight is in the law of the Lord, and on his law they meditate day and night.
2 : , lit., “recite”; ancients usually read aloud. , possibly the trees in the Temple garden (Pss 52.8; 92.12–15) on Mount Zion, abundantly watered, symbolizing vitality (Pss 46.4; 65.9; Ezek 47.12).
They are like trees planted by streams of water, which yield their fruit in its season, and their leaves do not wither. In all that they do, they prosper.
1–3 : Any individual who adheres to the torah ( ), the authoritative teaching contained in the psalms, and rejects the views of the wicked, is fortunate.
The wicked are not so, but are like chaff that the wind drives away.
4–7 : In contrast to the well‐watered and deeply rooted tree (a vertical image; cf. Jer 17.7–8) is the dry and wind‐driven (a horizontal image).
Therefore the wicked will not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous;
for the Lord watches over the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked will perish.
